subject: Geography GS
approach: Maximum depth — every examinable sub-concept gets full treatment
structure: MEGA_TOPIC > SUB_TOPIC > LEAF_TOPIC (each leaf = full 9-section content)

subtopics.title: Big Bang Theory and Origin of the Universe
depth_points: Singularity concept — infinite density, temperature
Expansion timeline: 10^-43 sec (Planck epoch) to present
Evidence: Cosmic Microwave Background Radiation (Penzias & Wilson 1965)
Hubble's Law — recession velocity proportional to distance
Red shift of galaxies as proof of expansion
Dark matter and dark energy — 95% of universe is unknown
Age of Universe: 13.8 billion years (WMAP/Planck satellite data)
Limitations of Big Bang — what it doesn't explain (before singularity)

subtopics.title: Theories of Solar System Formation
depth_points: Nebular Hypothesis (Kant 1755, Laplace 1796) — rotating gas cloud contraction
Planetesimal Hypothesis (Chamberlin & Moulton) — solid body accretion
Tidal Hypothesis (Jeans & Jeffreys) — passing star pulled material
Binary Star Hypothesis (Hoyle & Lyttleton)
Modern Solar Nebula Theory — combines nebular + planetesimal
Proto-planetary disk → accretion → differentiation sequence
Why inner planets are rocky, outer planets are gaseous
Bode's Law — mathematical spacing of planetary orbits

subtopics.title: Structure of the Solar System
depth_points: Sun — composition (H 74%, He 24%), nuclear fusion, solar wind
Terrestrial planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars — characteristics comparison
Jovian planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune — gas giants
Asteroid Belt — location, composition, Ceres (dwarf planet)
Kuiper Belt and Oort Cloud — cometary reservoir
Dwarf planets: Pluto, Eris, Makemake, Haumea — IAU 2006 reclassification
Earth's uniqueness — Goldilocks zone, magnetic field, atmosphere, water

subtopics.title: Age of the Earth and Radiometric Dating
depth_points: Earth's age: 4.54 billion years (Patterson 1956, meteorite dating)
Radiometric dating principles — parent/daughter isotope ratio
Uranium-Lead dating (4.5 Ga half-life) — for oldest rocks
Potassium-Argon dating (1.3 Ga half-life) — for volcanic rocks
Carbon-14 dating (5730 yr half-life) — for organic remains up to 50,000 years
Rubidium-Strontium dating — for metamorphic rocks
Oldest known rocks: Acasta Gneiss (4.03 Ga, Canada)
Oldest known minerals: Jack Hills zircons (4.4 Ga, Australia)
Indian context: Dharwar craton rocks >3.0 Ga, Singhbhum >3.3 Ga

subtopics.title: Geological Time Scale — Eons and Eras
depth_points: Hierarchy: Eon > Era > Period > Epoch > Age
Four Eons: Hadean (4.5-4.0 Ga), Archean (4.0-2.5 Ga), Proterozoic (2.5-0.54 Ga), Phanerozoic (0.54 Ga-present)
Hadean: magma ocean, heavy bombardment, no life, no rocks survive
Archean: first life (prokaryotes), BIFs, greenstone belts, no oxygen
Proterozoic: Great Oxidation Event (2.5 Ga), first eukaryotes, Snowball Earth
Phanerozoic: visible life explosion, three eras
Three Eras of Phanerozoic: Paleozoic (ancient life), Mesozoic (middle life), Cenozoic (recent life)
How to remember: 'Had Archie's Protein Pancake' (Hadean, Archean, Proterozoic, Phanerozoic)

subtopics.title: Geological Time Scale — Periods and Indian Geological Events
depth_points: Paleozoic Periods: Cambrian, Ordovician, Silurian, Devonian, Carboniferous, Permian
Cambrian Explosion (541 Ma) — rapid diversification of multicellular life
Carboniferous (359-299 Ma) — vast coal forests, Gondwana coal deposits in India
Permian-Triassic extinction (252 Ma) — 96% species lost, 'Great Dying'
Mesozoic Periods: Triassic, Jurassic, Cretaceous
Jurassic: dinosaur dominance, Gondwana breakup begins
Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction (66 Ma) — asteroid + Deccan volcanism
Cenozoic Periods: Paleogene, Neogene, Quaternary
Quaternary: Pleistocene Ice Ages, Holocene (human civilization), Anthropocene debate
Indian events mapped to time: Gondwana coal (Carboniferous-Permian), Deccan Traps (Late Cretaceous), Himalayan uplift (Eocene-present), Siwalik sediments (Miocene)

subtopics.title: Earth's Early Differentiation and Layer Formation
depth_points: Homogeneous accretion → heterogeneous differentiation (iron catastrophe)
Gravitational energy + radioactive heating → magma ocean stage
Heavy elements (Fe, Ni) sank to form core; light silicates rose to form mantle/crust
Core formation timeline: first 30-50 million years after accretion
Moon formation: Giant Impact Hypothesis (Theia collision, 4.5 Ga)
Evidence for Moon origin: lunar sample composition matches Earth's mantle
Earth's magnetic field generation begins with liquid outer core formation
Late Heavy Bombardment (4.1-3.8 Ga) — final accretion phase

subtopics.title: Evolution of Earth's Atmosphere
depth_points: Primary atmosphere: H2, He — lost to space (low gravity for light gases)
Secondary atmosphere: volcanic outgassing — CO2, N2, H2O, SO2, NH3
No free oxygen for first 2 billion years
Great Oxidation Event (2.4 Ga): cyanobacteria photosynthesis → O2 production
Banded Iron Formations (BIFs) as evidence of oxygen appearance
Ozone layer formation → enabled land colonization by life
Current composition: N2 (78%), O2 (21%), Ar (0.93%), CO2 (0.04%)
Carbon cycle regulation — geological thermostat via silicate weathering

subtopics.title: Evolution of Hydrosphere and Oceans
depth_points: Source of water: volcanic outgassing + cometary/asteroidal delivery
Ocean formation: ~4.4 Ga (evidence from Jack Hills zircons showing liquid water)
Early oceans: hot, acidic, iron-rich, no dissolved oxygen
Salinity buildup: weathering of continental rocks → dissolved minerals to ocean
Ocean volume has remained relatively constant for 3+ billion years
Role of oceans in CO2 regulation — carbon sink
Indian context: Tethys Sea (Mesozoic) → Himalayan sedimentary rocks today

subtopics.title: Formation of Continents — Cratons and Shield Areas
depth_points: First continental crust: TTG (tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite) formation in Archean
Craton = stable continental core (shield + platform)
Shield = exposed Precambrian crystalline basement
Platform = shield covered by younger flat-lying sediments
Global major cratons: Canadian Shield, Baltic Shield, Siberian Craton, Australian Craton
Indian cratons: Dharwar (Karnataka), Bastar (Chhattisgarh), Singhbhum (Jharkhand), Aravalli (Rajasthan), Bundelkhand (UP-MP)
Mineral wealth concentrated in cratons: iron (Singhbhum), gold (Dharwar), manganese (Bastar)
Craton stability → why Peninsular India has low seismicity vs Himalayan belt
